title: Reading and Use of English - Part 2
description: For questions 9 - 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Empathy, broadly defined as the capacity to understand and share the emotional states of others, is increasingly recognised as one of the most important components of social intelligence, (0) which shapes the quality of human relationships at every level. Its role in cooperative behaviour and moral decision-making has generated substantial scientific interest. (9) Although empathy was long treated as a single uniform capacity, researchers now distinguish between cognitive empathy and affective empathy, the latter involving the direct sharing of emotional experience. (10) Such is the importance of this distinction that failing to differentiate between the two can lead to contradictory conclusions about the relationship between empathy and prosocial behaviour. Clinical evidence supports the value of cognitive empathy in professional settings, and (11) Provided that practitioners maintain appropriate boundaries, the capacity to understand a patient's perspective without being overwhelmed by their distress is central to effective medical and therapeutic practice. Not (12) only does empathic accuracy improve outcomes in therapeutic relationships, but research has also shown that leaders who demonstrate perspective-taking skills tend to manage more cohesive and productive teams. (13) Regardless of whether empathy is predominantly innate or can be meaningfully cultivated through training, there is broad agreement that social environments profoundly shape its expression. Not (14) until longitudinal developmental studies began tracking empathic responses from infancy did researchers establish how early the capacity for emotional resonance with others begins to develop. (15) Throughout human history, philosophical and religious traditions have placed empathy at the centre of ethical life, though the term itself is relatively modern in origin. These traditions suggest its deep roots in human social organisation. (16) Whether empathy is regarded as a biological endowment, a learned skill or a moral virtue, its centrality to human flourishing is increasingly supported by both scientific research and everyday experience.

example.text: Empathy, broadly defined as the capacity to understand and share the emotional states of others, is increasingly recognised as one of the most important components of social intelligence, (0) which shapes the quality of human relationships at every level.

questions.explanation: Concessive conjunction used to introduce a contrast or concession between two clauses.
Emphatic determiner in the inverted structure 'Such was/is the [noun] that...' to express a high degree.
Conditional conjunction meaning 'on the condition that', used to introduce a condition and its result.
Part of the correlative conjunction 'Not only... but also', which requires subject-auxiliary inversion after 'Not only'.
Part of the fixed prepositional phrase 'Regardless of', meaning 'without being affected by'.
Part of the 'Not until' negative fronting structure, which requires subject-auxiliary inversion.
Preposition or adverb meaning 'all through a period of time' or 'from start to finish', used as a temporal discourse marker.
Conjunction introducing two alternatives or possibilities in a summarising conclusion.
